pattern: Reverse-graph topological elimination: a node is safe exactly when every successor is safe, so peel off terminal nodes and propagate safety backward.
hints: A node is safe when it cannot reach any cycle; terminal nodes with no outgoing edges are trivially safe.
Color each node white/grey/black with DFS, marking grey while on the current path so revisiting a grey node reveals a cycle.

Equivalently, reverse every edge and run a queue from nodes of out-degree zero, decrementing successors' out-degree and enqueuing any that reach zero; those reached are the safe nodes, returned sorted.

edge_inputs.description: mixed graph with a small cycle and several safe nodes
only the lone terminal node is safe
single node with a self-loop is unsafe
every node sits on one big cycle so none are safe
a chain feeding into a cycle leaves only the cycle-free tail unsafe

stress.description: random sparse directed graph with deliberate forward bias plus occasional back edges to create cycles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    graph = []
    for i in range(n):
        outs = set()
        deg = rng.randint(0, 3)
        for _ in range(deg):
            j = rng.randrange(n)
            outs.add(j)
        graph.append(sorted(outs))
    return [graph]
stress.note: random targets create a realistic mix of cycles and safe chains

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def eventualSafeNodes(self, graph):
        # Independent approach: per-node DFS memo where a node is safe iff every
        # successor is safe; a node still on the current stack (provisional False)
        # signals a cycle and stays unsafe.
        import sys
        sys.setrecursionlimit(100000)
        n = len(graph)
        safe = [None] * n
        def solve(u):
            if safe[u] is not None:
                return safe[u]
            safe[u] = False
            ok = True
            for v in graph[u]:
                if not solve(v):
                    ok = False
                    break
            safe[u] = ok
            return ok
        return [i for i in range(n) if solve(i)]
